Научный руководитель: старший преподаватель кафедры финансов и учета ВШМ СПбГУ, к.э.н. Андрианов Александр  Юрьевич . Тема данной  ВКР  — « Определение точности и корректировка оценки стоимости компаний сравнительным подходом на развивающихся рынках ». Целью данного исследования является определение наиболее точных мультипликаторов и критериев для выбора сопоставимых предприятий при оценке компаний на развивающихся рынках.  Задачи  включают следующее: Провести обзор современной литературы и анализ имеющихся исследований по этим вопросам. Определить специфику оценки компании с помощью сравнительного подхода и выявить особенности применяемых оценочных мультипликаторов. Определите наиболее часто используемые мультипликаторы и определит ь  методы их расчета. Определит ь  критерии выбора компаний-аналогов. Определит ь  наилучший метод корректировки  мультипликаторов . Проверить на практике использование различных мультипликаторов, критериев выбора компаний-аналогов и скорректировать результаты по компаниям из развивающихся рынков. Исслед овать  взаимосвязь между мультипликаторами, выбором критериев и точностью оценок. Основные результаты: Наиболее точные мультипликаторы основаны на выручке. Наименее точным  мультипликатором  является  P / B . Наиболее точным критерием для  выбора аналогов  является комбинированный критерий. Ключевые слова: оценка, публичные сопоставимые объекты, аналоги, мультипликаторы, критерии выбора, корректировка, методы коррекции, точность, погрешность . ANNOTATION This thesis is written by  GSOM  MCF  2nd year student, Ruslan Zhangirov.  Scientific director: Senior Lecturer, Department of Finance and Accounting, GSOM SPbU, PhD in Economics, Andrianov   Alexander.   The topic of this thesis is the “Determination of Accuracy and Adjustment of Company Valuation by Comparative Approach in Emerging Markets”. The  goal  of this study  is to identify the most accurate multiples and criteria for selecting comparable businesses when evaluating companies in the Emerging Markets.   Objectives  include the following : Conduct a review of current literature and analysis of available research on these issues. Determine the specifics of the company's valuation using a comparative approach and identify the features of the applied valuation multiples. Identify the most commonly used multiples and determine methods for their calculation. Determine the criteria for selecting peer companies. Determine the best method for adjusting multiples. Check in practice the use of various multiples, criteria for selecting peer companies, and adjust results on companies from  Emerging Markets . Investigate the relationship between the multiples, selecting criteria, and accuracy of estimates. Main results : The most accurate multiples are those based on revenue. The least accurate multiple is P/B The most precise criterion for multiples is  combined criteria . Keywords: valuation, public comparables, peers, multiples, selection criteria, adjustment, correction methods, accuracy, error. INTRODUCTION Rationale for the choice of topic. I chose this particular topic because, while working in business valuation, I noticed a lack of  understanding   regarding the  evaluati on   of  accuracy of multiples  and criteria and their applicability in EM . Relevance. The share of  Emerging Markets  in global GDP has increased by 10% from 2009   to   2019 1 .   The  choice  of valuation multiples  and  selection criteria can have a significant impact on investment decisions. Research in this area can help to reduce the risk of investment decisions based on inaccurate valuations . Object of the thesis: valuation of EM public companies .   Subject of the thesis: accuracy of company valuation by Comparative Approach in EM . Main goal. The aim of this study is to identify the  most accurate  multiples  and  criteria for selecting comparable businesses when  evaluating companies   in the   Emerging Markets . Objectives: Conduct a review of current literature and analysis of available research on these issues. Determine the specifics of the company's valuation using a comparative approach and identify the features of the applied valuation multiples. Identify the most commonly used multiples and determine methods for their calculation. Determine the criteria for selecting peer companies. Determine  the best  method for adjusting multiples. Check in practice the use of various multiples, criteria for selecting peer companies, and adjust  results  on companies from  Emerging Markets . Investigate the relationship between the multiples ,  selecting  criteria, and accuracy of estimates . Main hypotheses: The best selection criteria will be the combination of all criteria. The most accurate multiples will be those based on sales.   EV based multiples are more accurate than price based multiples.   Combined selection criteria are considered to be more accurate because   several criteria can help to reduce the impact of outliers and errors in the data. Sales multiples are considered to be more accurate because sales are less volatile than net earnings. EV-based multiples are considered to be more accurate  because they take into account a company's total capital structure, including debt and equity, while price-based multiples only consider the equity portion.   Research questions: What peer selection criteria are most appropriate (lead to the most accurate estimates) for companies in  emerging  market s ? Do sales multiples produce more accurate results ? Do  EV  multiples produce more accurate results ? Practical  i mplication. Investors can apply certain multiples  and   selection  criteria   to get the most accurate estimates of  emerging markets  companies. This will enable them to make profitable investment decisions. Theoretical   i mplication . This research could encourage scholars and practitioners to dive deeper into the topic and analyze the accuracy of different combinations on different markets in different time periods.  Moreover, the most accurate combinations of criteria and multiples could be further explored.  This could lead to more reliable  results  being discovered. Research l imitations. The results of an analysis based on only  3  countr ies  may not always be universal due to country specifics. Limited number of multiples, selection criteria, and correction methods were considered. Only one combination of selection criteria is considered. The accuracy of multiples-criteria combinations were not examined. A specific company is not compared by different multipliers with a fixed set of peer companies, since different criteria for selecting peer companies are used, which directly affect the choice of peers. This work consists of an introduction, theoretical part, practical part, conclusion and applications. The theoretical part provides literature reviews on business valuation, comparative method, specific multiples, selection criteria and correction methods. All reviews present strengths and weaknesses, features of the use of multiples, criteria and methods of correction. The research methodology is described. In the practical part, the results of the study are presented and discussed. THEORY  CHAPTER Problem statement Examining the role of accuracy  in  the comparative approach to company valuation is of particular relevance today for several reasons. One of these reasons is the current trend towards globalization of the world financial system 1  and the increase in the number of international investment transactions 1 , as well as the positive influence of foreign investments on the development and growth of national economies 1 . In recent years, investors have been particularly interested in   developing countries, the largest representative of which is the so-called BRICS bloc, which includes Brazil, the Russian Federation, India, China and South Africa. The underdevelopment of the stock market, the lack of a sufficient number of listed   companies and information about similar companies, as well as the existence of other specific limitations lead to some difficulties in evaluating the value of companies in emerging markets 3 .   Of course, when determining the value of the company, the existing social, political, financial and economic characteristics of these countries must not be ignored 3 . In this regard, a business  appraiser  or investment advisor evaluating a company in an emerging capital market needs a methodological framework that allows him or her to  make adjustments and  assess accuracy taking into account the risks and characteristics listed above. Therefore, the legitimacy of verifying the correctness of the comparative multiples stems from the existence   of an interest in investing in companies from emerging markets, and   the existence of specificities and threats  of  these markets, which must be expressed and taken into account. In the article “ An empirical study of country risk adjustments to market multiples valuation in emerging markets ” the author identifies a significant research gap in determining the best combinations of multiples  and selection  criteria for selecting comparables   in emerging markets 4 . While prior research has explored these issues individually, there has been limited research on how these factors interact within a valuation context  in EM . The author highlights the need for future research to focus on identifying the most effective  selection criteria  and   multiples  for selecting comparables   in emerging economies 4 .   Moreover,  previous papers have the following limitations and shortcomings: Research to date has been based on the study of multiple s  adjustment methods such as P/E and P/B, the application of which is often limited due to the financial situation of the surveyed companies or the specifics of the industry. This thesis focuses on  examining common and sufficiently universal multiples such as EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA. Previous empirical studies on the  accuracy  of comparison multiples for country risk were based on much older sample years. Since then, important structural changes have taken place both in the economies of developing countries and in the global economic balance. Previous studies were focused on one or two elements of comparables analysis:  multiples  or selection criteria 5 . In this thesis, such universal multiples as EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA were examined using the most recent data. To avoid the potentially distorting external factors and specific intercompany factors, the trend over time w as  considered. Therefore, analogues selection, multiples computation and adjustment, accuracy estimation will be implemented for several consecutive years (201 8 -20 22 ).  This period covers three crisis years (2019-2022), hence the research will also reflect the current trends and results could be applicable for the current environment. Moreover,  this period refers to the most recent data and is most relevant .  India, Russia and Brazil   are  chosen because, according to the article “ An empirical study of country risk adjustments to market multiples valuation in emerging markets ” there is a gap in corresponding research for public companies  in these countries 25 . One possible reason is the limited availability of reliable and accurate financial data on  these  companies, which may make it difficult to perform meaningful valuation analyses 5 . Another reason could be the lack of a standardized approach to valuation in  EM , which may lead to inconsistencies and subjectivity in valuation estimates 6 .  Moreover, according to The Balance, these three countries are among the largest emerging markets in the world 59 .   Additionally, the  EM  may be relatively underdeveloped compared to other markets, which could mean there are fewer experts and resources available to conduct research on these topics. Methodology Lists of randomly selected target companies  and corresponding financial parameters  for 3 countries (Russia, Brazil, India) were uploaded from the set of companies complying to filters  described later  using the InvestingPro platform.  Data represented in US dollars at the average annual rate for P&L indicators and at the exchange rate on the cut-off day for BS information in order to ensure accurate comparison.  Each country has the same number of target companies -  150. For each company, lists of public comparables from the US market are downloaded in accordance with certain selection criteria. When valuing businesses in emerging markets using the public comparables approach, developed market comparables are frequently preferred for a few important reasons 7 : Quality and abundance of data. Generally speaking, developed markets have more stringent reporting requirements and open financial reporting. As a consequence, there is more detailed information and higher-quality data available for comparative analysis. Data in developing marketplaces might be scarce or unreliable. Regulatory approval. Regulators, auditors, and other stakeholders are more apt to accept valuations based on comparables from developed markets. They are a tried-and-true method, whereas comparables from emerging markets might be subject to more examination regarding their dependability. Market cycles and security. Throughout history, developed markets have gone through numerous market cycles, booms, and crashes. This aids in choosing analogous businesses that have experienced comparable ups and downs, as well as in choosing the right valuation multiples. The research is less reliable because of how volatile and cyclical emerging markets can be. For a few crucial reasons, the US market is frequently used as a standard for the public comparables valuation approach 8 : Dimensions and money. According to market capitalization, the US stock market is the biggest equity market worldwide. This guarantees greater liquidity for analysis and makes it simpler to locate comparable public companies. Smaller markets might have fewer similar businesses and a lack of data. Standards for reporting and transparency. The SEC has established strict requirements for disclosure and transparency for US corporations. As a consequence, there is high-quality financial reporting and plenty of data for comparative analysis. Other markets might have lower reporting requirements. Emerging markets may have less transparent regulatory systems and less information available on public companies. Access to information. On US public businesses, a wealth of information is accessible, including annual reports, news articles, analyst reports, public databases, and more. Comparables research becomes more thorough and insight-driven as a result. There might be less information available in other areas. Regulatory approval. Since the public comparables approach is founded on the well-known US market, it is given more credence by regulators, auditors, and in legal proceedings. Comparables from other marketplaces might come under more scrutiny or have their applicability questioned. In total, 5 selection criteria were used, so for each company, 5 lists with public comparables were uploaded (1 list per 1 selection criteria). This procedure was done for each year from 2018 to 2022  because the value of a company is a constantly changing indicator. To avoid the potentially distorting external factors and specific intercompany factors, the trend over time will be considered. Therefore, analogues selection, multiples computation and adjustment, accuracy estimation will be implemented for several consecutive years (2018-2022).  Thus, for each target company, 25 lists with public comparables were uploaded (for each of the 5 years, 5 lists with public comparables were uploaded - 1 list per 1 selection criteria). Next, based on each list with public comparables, the average values of multiples for the list were calculated. The following multiples were used: EV/S, EV/EBITDA, P/S, P/E, P/B. Then, based on these average multiples, EV and Price for the target company were calculated by multiplying the average multiple (for example, the average P/S was multiplied by the target company's revenue or the average EV/EBITDA was multiplied by the company's target EBITDA). The prices and EV of comparable companies were recovered, respectively, at the end of April 2019–2023, from the years following the reporting data. This method of using market data is meant to guarantee the comparability and relevance of reporting data and market data. The applied approach is widely used in many empirical studies and. It typically takes companies four months to prepare and pass an audit of their financial statements 6 . During this time, investors are unable to clearly evaluate how well the companies performed the previous year. Further, errors were calculated (for example, deviations of the calculated Price from the real market capitalization). In  this   thesis , a specific company is not compared by different multiples with a fixed set of peer companies, since different criteria for selecting peer companies are used, which directly affect the choice of peers. To test the hypotheses, a two-way ANOVA test was performed  within JASP software .  The dependent variable is accuracy (error), independent – selection criteria and multiple. To test the first hypothesis, the mean errors of estimates based on the combined selection criteria were  compared with the mean errors based on other selection criteria. To test the second hypothesis, the mean errors of estimates based on the EV/S and P/S were compared with the mean errors based on other multiples. To test the third hypothesis, the mean errors of estimates based on the EV/S and EV/EBITDA were compared with the mean errors based on other multiples. Thus, 3 ANOVA tests were performed (1 for each country). In each of the 3 tests, errors were taken for all multiples for all years for all target companies and for all criteria (150 target companies * 5 years * 5 multiples * 5 criteria = 18750 errors for each country).  The Two-way factorial  ANOVA  method is particularly useful  in this case because we have 2 independent  categorical  and 1 dependent  numerical  variable.  Moreover, ANOVA  allows us to examine the effects of each independent variable on  t he dependent variable while controlling for the effects of the other independent variable. This can help us to better understand the unique contributions of each independent variable to the dependent variable. ANOVA tests have assumptions which must be checked: Categorical independent variables.  Groups must have equal sample sizes.  Population variances must be equal (i.e. homoscedastic). Normality: the dependent variable is normally distributed. To test for homogeneity, the Levene test was applied. This test is a commonly used statistical test that is used to assess the equality of variances across groups. It is particularly useful in the context of two-way factorial ANOVA because it allows us to test for the equality of variances across groups defined by the levels of the two independent variables.  Additionally, it is less sensitive to outliers than other tests of equality of variances, such as the F-test. Q - Q  plot was used to check for normality. A Q-Q plot (quantile-quantile plot) is a commonly used graphical method to assess the normality of a distribution. It is particularly suitable for checking for normality because it provides a visual comparison between the observed data and the expected values of a normal distribution. The Q-Q plot is a useful tool for checking for normality because it can detect departures from normality that might not be apparent from other methods, such as a histogram or a normal probability plot. Additionally, the Q-Q plot is more robust to small sample sizes and outliers than other methods. Custom Contrasts and post hoc tests were used to examine specific differences of means among criteria and multiples.   Custom contrasts can be used to test specific hypotheses or comparisons that are not addressed by the main effects or interaction effects in a two-way  ANOVA. Post-hoc tests, on the other hand, are used to examine the significant differences between groups or levels after a significant main effect or interaction effect has been found.  The use of custom contrasts and post-hoc tests in two-way ANOVA can enhance the interpretability of the results and provide more detailed information about the relationship between the independent variables and the dependent variable. By testing specific hypotheses or comparisons and identifying significant differences  between groups  or levels, these methods can help to provide a more nuanced understanding of the effects of the independent variables on the dependent variable, and can lead to more targeted and meaningful conclusions.  Tukey's HSD (Honestly Significant Difference) was used as a post hoc test. It is a commonly used post-hoc test in two-way factorial ANOVA that is well-suited for identifying significant differences between groups or levels. Tukey's HSD is particularly useful in two-way factorial ANOVA because it is a conservative test that controls for the overall risk of Type I error (false positives) when multiple comparisons are being made. Tukey's HSD is also useful because it provides a single value that can be used to compare all possible pairs of means. This can be more efficient than other post-hoc tests that require multiple comparisons to be made. Filtering These filters apply to the selection of target companies.  Companies with a negative value -  net  loss ,  negative EBITDA, and negative equity for  201 8 -20 22  were excluded from the sample due to the  multiples  traits  when analyzing  combinations with  corresponding  multiples. Companies without classification data in accordance with the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) in terms of determining industry affiliation  were excluded from the sample due to the inability to identify the industry. Also, the availability of financial statements produced in accordance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) was a requirement for inclusion in the sample for companies from  EM  capital market s (Russia, Brazil, India) . Due to accounting for income and spending items of enterprises in accordance with national accounting systems, this selection criterion minimized potential distortions and incompatibility of data for companies from emerging market. Companies should have available data on financial statements for the period from  2018  to 20 22  inclusive. Companies must have positive values of all multiples. Companies used in the sample must have share price data for the period  April 2019 to April 2023 inclusive . The main assumption of the work is that all statements were published, and the share prices reflected the information provided on the financial position of the companies. Valuation Many issues may be resolved by determining the worth of any item, including a business. Nonetheless, there are a number of crucial circumstances in which it is essential to carry out a firm valuation. First, in order to determine the value of a share or block of shares, investment consultants and stock market participants must conduct an assessment. This is true whether they are preparing a company for an IPO, spin-off, M&A, or performing an annual revaluation of  equity  investments for IFRS purposes. Evaluation is done in connection with M&A transactions in order to determine the acceptable range of the transaction price 9 .   Also, information on the business's worth in the case of litigation, the settlement of shareholder disagreements, and the calculation of employee compensation in the context of the option motivation program must be obtained. Last but not least, firm value is the cornerstone of contemporary corporate finance, and managers' primary duties include maximizing it 10 . A. Damodaran, a well-known expert on the subject of assessment, names five crucial methods for the evaluation 11 : • The value of an asset should be determined by studying the price of comparable assets and linking it to some variable (for instance, income, cash flows, book value, or sales volume), according to the first technique, also known as a comparative valuation; • The  second  of them, discounted cash flow (DCF) valuation, compares the present value of anticipated future cash flows attributable to an asset to the asset's value; • The third strategy, contingent claims valuation, employs an option pricing model to estimate the worth of assets that resemble options. Income ,  cost , and comparative  approaches  are the three main methods used in practice to assess a company's worth 12 . The worth of a corporation is determined by the cost of its assets, including its financial assets, as determined using the cost method. This technique does not allow for the consideration of additional indications that are not accounted for on the organization's balance sheet, such as the company's standing in the marketplace, established supply and distribution routes, and other  indicators. This method is most frequently applied to the valuation of businesses that are in the process of bankruptcy or liquidation, as well as in cases involving certain capital-intensive economic sectors. In this sense, compared to other approaches, the value of the firm gained by using this strategy is frequently the least 13 . The income method makes the underlying assumption that a company's worth is the total of its discounted future cash flows. The key benefit of this strategy is the capacity to assess the intrinsic value that each firm carries by itself. This approach's principal drawbacks include subjectivity in estimating the company's future cash flows and costs, as well as significant sensitivity to important model parameters, such the discount rate and embedded macroeconomic projections. Yet, because it provides for accounting for the unique characteristics of each unique organization as well as information that is not generally accessible, this technique allows for the possibility of the most accurate cost estimate 13 . The comparative method is predicated on the idea of the law of one price, which states that when assessing the value of an asset, an investor starts from the value of comparable enterprises in terms of economic and financial metrics that are already available on the market 13 . In the context of the comparative approach, it is typical to distinguish between three basic methodologies: peer company approach deal strategy industry coefficients The foundation of the industry coefficients technique is the idea that the important financial, economic, and operational variables that are specific to each industry are correlated with the value of the firm. These indicators could include monthly or yearly profit, sales volume, inventory volume, equipment cost, or a mix of the aforementioned elements. Due to the lack of an active market for the acquisition and sale of businesses and the essential statistical data on industry investment transactions, this strategy is not as commonly employed in emerging capital markets as it is in Western practice. The peer-to-peer approach uses specialized valuation ratios (multiples) of companies whose shares are listed on the stock market and whose values are therefore publicly available to be calculated and used. It is feasible to ascertain the estimated worth of the firm being appraised relative to the market after assembling a group of peer companies and computing the average value of their multipliers 14 . The transaction approach entails using market data on the purchase price of a business or a block of shares during already concluded investment transactions. The key benefits of this approach are the ability to ascertain the exact price paid for a comparable financial asset and the premium the buyer paid for gaining control of the business. This method is frequently employed in appraisal for M&A purposes. The closeness and deficiency of the information on completed transactions that is currently available, the change in the market's economic situation and, consequently, the applicability of previous estimates, and the absence of transactions on assets that are truly comparable to the company being valued are the drawbacks of this approach 14 . The method of estimating using multiples is common and often utilized worldwide. Therefore, 96% of individuals questioned by the global consulting firm KPMG among 46 organizations in the investment and consulting industry employ this method when evaluating companies 15 . Despite the method's apparent simplicity, there may be a number of challenges involved in comparative valuation, which might ultimately lead to major distortions in the value determination. Such distortions may manifest as a result of improper peer company selection or the use of an appropriate multiple 15 . The ability and credentials of the analyst doing the valuation play a significant role in determining a company's worth. Not surprisingly, M. Kim and D. Ritter reach the conclusion that the employment of standardized algorithms is less effective than the work of qualified appraisers in their study on the examination of the value of firms going public 16 . The market's misperception of peer firms and the human element are the second potential cause of distortions. The fundamental tenet of the comparative method is that since the market as a whole appropriately assesses every company operating in a certain industry, the only factors affecting multiple s  are the particulars and degree of differentiation among the businesses. As the analyst can only be certain that the firms are neither overpriced or, on the contrary, undervalued relative to one another in this scenario, the assumption of this technique is based on the premise of a proper fundamental analysis by the market of companies that are analogs of the sample 16 . It is important to note the issue with stock market investors' methods and conduct in this respect. It is common knowledge that the markets experience significant swings and long-term trends, which may result in the development of so-called "bubbles in the stock market" or, conversely, in the exodus of investors from particular economic sectors. From the perspective of a company's value, these events may strongly boost or, on the other hand, significantly diminish a  company's capitalization. Investors may act in this manner in regard to the whole stock market or in regard tocertain sectors and businesses. Comparative valuation includes estimating a company's worth in relation to its competitors. Based on investor behavior trends, this value might range greatly from the company's intrinsic value, since investors may significantly overvalue or undervalue the entire group of comparable companies 16 . Types and classification of comparative multiples The multiple is essentially an estimated coefficient obtained by dividing the value of peer companies by certain financial and economic indicators. The resulting value can be multiplied by the existing value of the company being valued to get the required value.   Various classifications of multiples can be made - depending on the essence of the indicators used in the denominator - natural or financial, and also depending on the classification of the financial data itself - multiples based on indicators of profit, as well as values ​​of balances or money 17 . Natural multiples are used in the denominator of economic activity indicators specific to the industry or group of industries to which the company being rated belongs. Such indicators can be: the amount of available storage space (m2) for retail, the volume of stocks (tons) - for the commodity sector, the number of subscribers (people) for the telecom industry, etc. Typical examples of natural indicators can be EV/capacity or EV/reserves 17 . The main disadvantage of these   multiples is the impossibility of using diversified companies as analogues, as well as the secrecy of public information about physical indicators. One of the sources of such information can be company annual reports, as well as industry reports and trade publications.  F inancial multiples, on the other hand, lack these shortcomings: by default, the analogues of the rated companies are companies whose shares are traded on the stock exchange and whose data are relatively publicly available. In addition, in most countries of the world, the necessary condition for access to the stock market is the regular provision and free accessibility of financial statements, the accuracy of which must be certified by an audit report.  F inancial ratios are sufficiently universal and can be used both when evaluating diversified companies and companies in related sectors of the economy, which is often impossible when using highly specialized natural multiples 17 . Since the ultimate goal of benchmarking is to determine the value of the company of interest relative to the group of analog publicly traded companies, the median value of analog companies in the numerator of the multiple is used. This value can be the price (P - Price) of a stock or of the entire company, as well as the value of the enterprise value (EV - Enterprise Value)   18 . The value of companies (EV) is somewhat indicative of the amount a potential buyer is willing to pay to acquire complete control of the companies   and is calculated as the sum of the company's market capitalization, the market value of total debt, the value of preferred stock outstanding less the entity's cash and cash equivalents.   Based on the denominator values ​​used, financial multiples can be divided into the following categories 18 : Multiples based on corporate cash flows ; Multiples based on the company's income statement (PL) ; Balance-based multiples . Multiples with P&L indicators can be conditionally divided into “profitable multiples” and multiples with profitability indicators. "Earnings multiples" are common and use the company's sales figures as the denominator.  These multiples include P/S (price/earnings) and EV/S (enterprise value/earnings). These multiples have a number of advantages over others. First, the sales multiples are based on the sales indicator, which is a universal financial characteristic for all industries and can be used when comparing companies in more than one economic sector 18 . Second, earnings cannot be negative, which automatically eliminates many of the problems associated with win-based multiples. Third, unlike earnings, revenue is less subject to cyclical fluctuations, and information about it is easy to get from open sources.   Fourth,  revenue  is an indicator the least affected by the specifics of national accounts, which allows using multiples based on it without additional adjustments 18 .  O perational efficiency metrics can be conditionally classified as row-based metrics below the revenue metric, such as:  o perating result (EBIT), earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA) or net income. Let's consider the advantages and disadvantages of the most popular multiples based on the company's income statement: EV / EBITDA and P / E. The advantage of using these multiples is the universality of the indicators compared, which   provides a comparative analysis of companies from more than one branch, as well as the simplicity in interpreting the calculated coefficient 18 .  In fact, these multiples show how many indicators of accounting profits investors rate for a given company. The main benefit of EV/EBITDA is its independence from the capital structure of the companies being evaluated, which allows companies with different levels of debt to be compared. Also, this multiple allows  investors  to even out the depreciation dates based on the denominator, which is especially useful when valuing capital-intensive companies 18 .   The P/E multiple, despite its wide popularity, does not have these advantages. It should be noted that the company's profit is often negative, making it difficult to evaluate similar companies or leading to similar companies  being excluded from the sample altogether. A negative net profit ratio can appear in companies both due to operational difficulties - economic cycles, business problems and state of crisis - as well as in connection with a certain stage   of the life cycle - most companies in the early stages or in growth suffer losses in accounting terms 18 .   In addition, there is a tendency in some countries to intentionally underestimate and misstate accounting profits, mainly for tax purposes 19 . The above characteristics of the P/E multiple impose certain limitations on its widespread use. It emphasizes that the numerator and denominator of these multiples must match, depending on the potential availability of the distribution of cash flows attributable to their potential recipients. In other words, the ratios of the multiples must take into account the capital structure of the company, hence the EV/E multiple is meaningless since the net profits only available for distribution to the shareholders (the company's equity, not debt) 18 .   The following is a summary of the key multiples based on various metrics of the company's cash flow: • Price/Cash Earnings (P/CE); • Price/Net Cash Flow from Operations (P/OpFCF); • Price/Net Cash Flow to Shareholders (P/FCFE).   Despite the intuitive notion of the superiority of cash flow measures over accounting results, the latter allow for a more accurate assessment 18 . Finally, the last block are multiples based on the book value (in some cases the market value) of the company's assets 18 : • “Share Price/Book Value of Equity” or “Share Price/Book Value of Net Assets” (P/B – P/BVE); • “Enterprise value/value of buildings, machinery and equipment” (EV/PP&E); • "Enterprise Value/Book Value of Fixed Assets" (EV/BVA); • “Stock Price/Net Replacement Cost” (Tobin's Q).   This group of multiples has its own details and advantages. The main advantage of these multiples is their convenience for evaluating capital-intensive industries, as well as companies whose assets are tangible (including financial ones). Therefore, the P/B multiple is often used when evaluating companies in the financial sector: banks, insurance companies and leasing  companies, among others. It also makes sense to use this multiple when evaluating companies with large inventories in   warehouses 18 . However, this group of multiples is not free from limitations and shortcomings. First, they don't apply to companies whose business value - reputation, presence of a wide customer network, experience of employees, etc. - is not reflected on the balance sheet. In addition, balance sheet ratios largely depend on the characteristics of national accounting systems, as well as the duration of the ongoing revaluation of assets. And if the company is losing for different periods, the cost of capital can be negative 18 . In conclusion of this  chapter:  d espite the apparent outward simplicity, it brings with it a number of specific points and difficulties. Among the examples considered, EV/S and EV/EBITDA multiples are partially universal and offer the most benefits when assessing the value of companies using a comparative approach. Multiples  and  their features   Valuing companies in emerging markets can be challenging due to limited information and the volatility of these markets. As a result, traditional valuation methods such as discounted cash flow analysis may not be as effective. Instead, multiples based on comparable companies can be useful in valuing companies in emerging markets 20 . The industry in which the firm operates, the goal of the valuation, and the analyst's personal preferences may all influence the multiples that are used. The stage of the company's life cycle and its current profitability are important considerations, so M. Dang   advises choosing multipl e s carefully for businesses that are in the early stages of their life cycles or are in decline. In such cases, in his opinion, it is preferable to use balance sheet or revenue indicators 21 . A. Damodaran expresses a similar viewpoint :  for businesses with negative earnings, he advises using the aforementioned indicators, as negative values of the P/E multiple s  might exacerbate assessment issues 22 . Yet, there is disagreement over the clear benefit of utilizing particular multipl es . In his study, P. Fernandez finds that European analysts frequently utilize multiples based on the value of the enterprise (EV) - EV / EBITDA, EV / EBIT, EV / CF, as well as indicators Price /Revenue (P/S) 23 . Nevertheless, this list is not full. Experts from the McKinsey, M. Godhart, T. Kohler, and D. Wessels, also point out the preference for using multiples based on the enterprise value (EV), in particular EV / EBITDA, as this type of multiple does not depend on the capital structure, as opposed to  multiples  based on accounting profits 24 . Existing academic work concludes that multiples are unequally effective in determining the value of a company across different sectors of the economy. In this context, efficiency means the deviation of the value achieved by applying a certain multiple from the actual capitalization of the company. For example, in the work of M. Kim and J. Ritter, multiples for evaluating the share price of companies were analyzed.   Compared to P/E, P/B, P/S, EV/S, the EV/EBITDA multiple had the best statistical properties and the P/E value based on future earnings values ​​proved to be the most accurate 16 .   M. Baker and R. Rubak attempted to determine the best multiple for valuation and analyzed the effectiveness of multiples based on operating profit before amortization (EBITDA), operating profit (EBIT) and value of income. The results of the study showed that EBITDA-based industry multiples have the most effective values 25 . R. Lane and D. Reingold, studying the American market of various sectors of the economy, concluded that the P/E multiple is effective in valuing companies in the service sector, EV/A - for companies in the financial sector. and for a number of other sectors, for example communications or manufacturing - EV/EBITDA 26 . Other authors come to similar conclusions in their studies. In their article, E. Li e  and X. Li e  examined the comparative performance of various multiples based on sales, earnings and balance sheet values ​​for companies in different industries. The results show, as in the previous authors' work, the high efficiency of using the EV/book value multiple versus EV/sales, EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT for companies in the financial sector.   The authors find more significant results for measures based on EBITDA than on EBIT, with the exception of companies in the medical sector. It is also worth emphasizing the high efficiency of multiples based on the index of expected profits 27 .   In the works of J. Lew, D. Nissim and J.Thomas considered a variety of multiples, including the use of cash flow from operations (OCF). To construct the projected P/E ratios, the authors used analysts' assumptions about future earnings per share (EPS) and the growth rate of that ratio. After conducting the research, the authors came to the following conclusions regarding the effectiveness of different types of multiples: The most effective multiples were those based on expected future earnings values, then historical book profits, and then ratios calculated based on cash flows from operations and balance sheet. Equity Value (P/BV). The multiples using revenue reporting were the worst in terms of effectiveness 28 . In later papers, the same authors question the superiority of cash flow and dividend-based multiples over others. Theoretically, using such multiples can eliminate the problem of comparing companies with the same turnover and profit but different investment ratios. Unfortunately, as in their previous work, the researchers concluded that these multipliers are not efficient and found  that revenue forecasting multiples work best for larger companies.   A. Schreiner comes to similar conclusions regarding the higher effectiveness of predictive multiples compared to historical multiples 29 .   According to him, multiples based on historical values ​​have the following hierarchy in terms of valuation accuracy: those based on accounting profit metrics (P/E, P/EBT, P/EBIT, and P/EBITDA) are most effective  and followed by  cash flow and balance sheet  metrics . In another joint document with K. Spremann, he states that more accurate valuation results were obtained based on accounting profit multiples (P/E) than those based on enterprise value (EV/EBITDA or EV/EBIT) 29 .  The relative effectiveness of some multiples in emerging markets is analyzed in the work of S. Segal. In collaboration with A. Pandey, the author examines companies in thirteen key sectors of the Indian economy and concludes that P/E is the most effective compared to other analyzed multiples (P/CF, P /BV, sales P/E) 30 . In addition, the authors believe that P/E is the most meaningful under the conditions of India's emerging markets. In another article, the authors analyze the correctness of using multiples by BRICS member countr ies , with the exception of Russia. The effectiveness of combinations of multiples in assessing enterprise value is also examined. The results obtained show that it is more beneficial to use net earnings per share for Brazil and South Africa and balance sheet multiples for China  and  India. The hypothesis of a higher efficiency of the combinations of multiples compared to the individual values ​​was not confirmed 30 . Despite this, many authors conclude that using combined ratio-based multiples is effective. A. Cheng and R. McNamara suggest in their paper to use a combination of P/E and P/BV for best results 31 . In the cited work, A.Schreiner offers many types of weighting combinations for multiples of different vintages, different types and average types. He concludes that such an approach is effective when valuing companies in the oil and gas and banking sectors, and that it is better to use separate multiples for utilities and telecom companies 29 . P. Harbula also underlines the strong predictive power of multiple combinations based on balance sheet and accounting results 32 . Some authors focus their attention on research  of  the effectiveness of using multiples for firms in specific countries. So J.J. Lee and Y. Park examine the P/CF, P/B, P/E, P/S ratios for the Japanese stock market and come to conclusions about the different efficiencies of multiples depending on whether they belong to a particular economic sector.   During the stock market decline in Japan, the P/E multiple proved to be the most effective; conversely, the P/ C F ratio of the food and textile industries was more favorable. The P/B multiple provided the best estimates for all sectors except food 33 .   W. Hermann and F. Ritscher conducted a study on the effectiveness of  different multiples for European companies in the non-financial sector.   T he group of indicators analyzed included multiples such as goodwill/operating income before interest but after tax (EV/EBIAT), goodwill/operating income before interest, amortization after tax (EV/EBIDAAT), cost/capital investment (EV/DORT). However, the authors conclude that the most effective multiples are those based on book profits and the worst are those based on  revenue . The authors also note that P/B is more efficient than EV/EBIDAAT in selecting peers based on return on equity (ROE) and expected earnings growth rates 34 . The prior fiscal year, the previous 12 months, the following 12 months, etc. may all be related to market multiples. Time periods must match the denominator for accuracy, and the value in the nominator must match the value on the valuation date, which is usually the last day of the period. Applying multiples is a simple process: the amount of assets or relevant earnings stream of the valuation topic is multiplied by the computed market multiple, which must be from the same time period as the multiple. Any difference drastically skews the results. It's critical to pick the appropriate multiples for the valuation procedure when estimating firms. The industry, company stage, and particular characteristics of the firm being studied all influence the multiples that are used 35 .  Because various businesses have different traits and financial data that are important to their value, the choice of valuation multiples varies by industry. Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and Price-to-Sales (P/S) multiples, for instance, are frequently used in the technology sector, but Price-to-Net Asset Value (P/NAV) and Price-to-Funds from Operations (P/FFO) multiples are frequently used in the real estate sector 35 .  Because various multiples are applicable at different phases of a business's growth, the choice of valuation multiples relies on the stage of development of the firm. Price-to-Sales (P/S) multiples, on the other hand, may be more pertinent for early-stage firms with significant growth potential but limited profitability. As an illustration, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiples may be more pertinent for established companies with steady earnings. Moreover, for businesses with large levels of debt or capital expenditures, Enterprise Value-to-EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) multiples could be more pertinent 35 .  Because it serves as a benchmark for comparison with present and projected performance, the choice of valuation multiples is influenced by the company's previous performance. It's crucial to compare current multiples to historical norms and to peers when determining a company's intrinsic worth. For instance, a company may be overvalued if its Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple  is higher than its historical average. On the other side, a firm may be undervalued if its P/E multiple is lower than the historical average 35 .  Because market conditions can affect the multiples that are pertinent for a certain firm, the choice of valuation multiples is dependent on the state of the market. For instance, Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiples may be greater during a time of economic expansion because market confidence and earnings expectations have improved. In contrast, P/E multiples may be lower during an economic crisis since investor confidence has declined and profits forecasts have fallen 35 .  Market circumstances may also have an influence on the choice of similar businesses for multiples analysis. Finding comparable firms with comparable financial indicators could be more challenging during a time of market turbulence, which could affect the valuation's accuracy. The choice of valuation multiples is influenced by the company's future growth possibilities, since it affects the prospective profits and cash flows of the business. For instance, because investors are ready to pay more for the possibility of future earnings, a firm with strong growth prospects may have a higher Price-to-Earnings (P/E) ratio. Similar to the last example, investors are more ready to pay more for future cash flows, thus a firm with strong growth prospects may have a larger enterprise value-to-EBITDA (EV/EBITDA) multiple. When choosing multiples, it's crucial to take the company's growth prospects into account because they have an influence on the valuation's accuracy 35 . After selecting and calculating the analog company's multiples, the analyst must average the values ​​to arrive at the fair value for the company being valued 19 .  In his paper, Schreiner shows that the arithmetic mean of predicts the value of the company's shares worse and overestimates the real value 19 .  In his paper, S. Antonios concludes that using the arithmetic mean usually gives a downward deviation and the median gives an upward deviation. In general, according to Antonios, using the median and the arithmetic mean gives about the same error 36 . E.   Chirkova, when choosing between the arithmetic mean and the median, recommends using the latter, since it eliminates the effect of extreme values ​​​​leading to an upward or downward trend in the estimate. Otherwise, it makes sense to discard outliers when calculating the mean 37 .   In the mentioned works by A. Alford, M.   Richter, the authors come to similar conclusions regarding the accuracy of using the arithmetic mean .  The disadvantage of this approach is the  equal weighting of all values 38 . To neutralize the sampling error, the authors also suggest using the median.  M.  Thompson concluded that using harmonic means ​​is effective 39 . E. Maug and I. Dittmann conclude that the values that are least susceptible to errors and biases are obtained by using the median and geometric means after conducting a special study to determine the best operator for averaging multiples out of four: arithmetic mean, median, geometric, and harmonic means 40 . My   thesis i s based on the comparative multiples of companies from emerging capital market s  – Russia,  India, Brazil  as well as the benchmark market - the United States. The final  data set   was  1 1 . 250   data samples ,  based on  450  target companies   from  EMs  and   comparable companies from the USA selected for every target company .  Among them, 1 50  companies from Russia,  150  companies from Brazil, and 150 companies from India.   Every comparable set was selected based on 5 criteria and every year (201 8 -20 22 ). Therefore, there are 5 sets for each of 5 years or 25 samples for each target company .  Descriptive statistics for the above multiples are given in  the notes. Th is   thesis  takes into account  five  multiples, including P/S (price-to-sales), P/E (price-to-earnings), P/BV (price-to-book value), EV/EBITDA (the enterprise value ratio), and EV/Sales. These are the most common multiples used in valuation, according to McKinsey research 24 . Moreover, the Enterprise value-to-EBITDA ratio can be useful in valuing companies in emerging markets, as it accounts for a company's debt and provides a more comprehensive view of its financial health 24 . A study by Officer (2007) found that EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT multiples generate lower valuation errors than P/E ratio when using public comparables 41 . A study by Bhojraj and Lee (2002) found that EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT multiples outperform P/E ratio and other equity-based multiples in terms of valuation accuracy 42 . A study by Chan et al. (2001) also found that EV/EBITDA and EV/EBIT multiples produce more accurate estimates than P/E ratio when using public company comparables 43 .   Also, t hese  set of multiple s  cover both price and EV  using different financial parameters . Calculations of the value of companies using multiples are made according to the following formula (Formula 1): P̂ i,t  – the estimated value of the firm being valued (share value or EV) . V i,τ  – value driver of the company being valued (depending on the multiple used) . P j,t  – peer company value (share value or EV) . V j,τ  – peer value driver (depending on the multiple used) . Selection criteria The selection of peer firms is the focus of this section.  Various factors can serve as a guide for choosing analogues, but one of the key factors is the similarity in the form of the company's economic activity. Equality of goods manufactured or services provided is desirable, but it is considered acceptable to sample companies with similar products in the same industry if the manufactured goods or services experience the same cyclical fluctuations as the tested company's products. Equally important is the concurrence of   other key criteria related to the implementation process: final consumer of goods, focus of sales markets, distribution channels, geographic location and business segmentation 42 .   The financial characteristics of the sampled companies include size, profitability, growth potential and the creditworthiness of their peers. Company size is generally understood in terms of market capitalization, assuming similar underlying market multiples for similarly sized companies.   Profitability indicators can include both P & L data and indicators calculated from balance sheet values ​​(ROA, ROE, ROIC). A company's ability to pay is determined by its ability to borrow and its current debt load 42 . Ratings from specialized agencies can be used as a measure to determine solvency, e.g.   Moody's, Standard & Poor's, Fitch etc. A. Alford tests different approaches to selecting peer companies for the P/E multiple assessment, using a combination of different financial characteristics of the company in addition to industry affiliation criteria. Such characteristics are book profit growth rate, value of return on assets (ROE) and size of assets. Their results indicate that the most accurate estimate is provided by the characteristics of being in the same industry, as well as a combination of ROE and asset size 39 . At the same time, the author of another study, K.Skosvik testing the return on investment and its growth rate as a criterion for selecting analog ue s, conclude that the evaluation results obtained are highly biased. In his opinion, the best criterion should be the uniform affiliation of the analog ue s to the industry of the company being evaluated, paying special attention to the specific investment risks and accounting principles of the companies 43 . A study by R. Rubak and S. Kaplan conduct s  an assessment using the discounted cash flow method and concludes that using the industry factor and involving similar companies in similar transactions allows comparable values ​​of enterprise value to be obtained 44 . The comparable companies should be in the same industry as the company being analyzed. This is important because companies in different industries may have different growth prospects, profit margins, and risk profiles.  There is a large body of academic research on the most effective criteria for peer selection.  According to Alford, A.W., the industry (IND) SIC or GICS firm code  should be  employed as a selection criterion 38 .  The author  of  this  study tested the effectiveness of the rating using a P/E multiple based on a sample of industry peers. Their results indicate an improvement in valuation accuracy when selecting companies from the same industry with similar historical earnings growth rates 38 . However, there are studies whose results indicate the existence of more effective criteria for selecting peer companies. S.  Bhojraj  and S. Lee test the selection factors  of  multiples constructed using regression equations. Using the fundamental financial metrics of similar companies, such as projections of future earnings growth or levels of debt, the authors conclude that these selection factors are relatively more effective than industry affiliation 42 .   V. Herrmann and F. Richter come to similar conclusions, according to the results of their work, indicators such as the growth rate of net profit and return on investments are the most effective selection criteria 34 . Alford, A.W. used three fundamental criteria to choose the organizations for comparison: risk, growth potential, and company breadth 38 . This thesis uses the following criteria: Industry .   Comparable businesses from the same sector as the  target  business were chosen for each  target  business. Size .  The comparable companies should be of similar size to the company being analyzed in terms of revenue, market capitalization, and other financial metrics. This is important because larger companies may have different growth prospects and risk profiles than smaller ones. Enterprise Size is determined by the size of the company's  Market Cap , as defined in Alford, A.W.  Market cap takes into account both the total number of shares outstanding and the current market price of each share, providing a more accurate picture of a company's overall value than other measures of size, such as revenue or assets. Market cap is a forward-looking measure of a company's potential growth and profitability 38 .  In this manner, comparable businesses are chosen. Growth prospects .  The comparable companies should have similar growth prospects to the company being analyzed. This is important because companies with different growth prospects may have different valuation multiples.  Company growth can be measured in several ways, including by its sales dynamics, which is the rate at which a company's sales revenues are growing  over time. There are several reasons why sales dynamics may be considered the best measure of a company's growth. Firstly, sales dynamics provide a clear and objective measure of a company's performance over time 45 . By tracking a company's sales revenues, investors and analysts can easily see whether the company is growing or contracting, and by how much. Secondly, sales dynamics are a forward-looking measure of a company's potential for future growth. Companies that are experiencing strong sales growth are generally seen as having a greater potential for future success than companies with declining sales 45 . Thirdly, sales dynamics are a good indicator of a company's ability to compete in its industry. Companies with strong sales growth are likely to be gaining market share and/or expanding into new markets, indicating that they are successfully competing against their rivals 45 .  I chose  comparables  whose  1 year sales growth  figures are as near as possible to the  target companies figures . The number of analog businesses is chosen in this way. Profitability .  The comparable companies should be similarly profitable to the company being analyzed in terms of gross margin or operating margin or net income margin. This is important because companies with different levels of profitability may have different valuation multiples 42 . Although there are other kinds of profit margins, net profit margin is the most important and widely used one 45 .  N et profit  is the company's bottom line after all other costs, such as taxes and one-time expenses. Creditors, investors, and companies themselves utilize profit margins as gauges of a company's financial stability, management aptitude, and potential for expansion. Therefore, in this research, the net profit margin is used for selection of comparables. Industry+profitability+growth+size .   The features of being a part of one industry, along with a combination of  growth , size and profitability, provide the most accurate assessment, according to the author's research.  A study by Chan et al. (2001) used industry, size, and growth similarity to select comparables and found that using multiple criteria produces more accurate valuation estimates than using industry similarity alone 43 . A study by Officer (2007) used business model, size, and growth similarity and found that combining multiple criteria leads to lower valuation errors than individual criteria 41 . A study by Bhojraj and Lee (2002) used industry, size, profitability, and growth similarity and found that a weighted combination of multiple criteria produces the most accurate valuation estimates 42 . These selection criteria are most often mentioned in the works of researchers such as Alford, A.W. They broadly cover the risk and growth metrics required for peer selection. Adjustment methods Making an adjustment that can account for nation risk is the last stage in applying the multiple technique to determine the value of a firm from an emerging market. According to a poll by the PwC, the great majority of analysts (88%) use these modifications when analyzing firms in  developing nations . T his method has become relatively common in global practice 46 . Commercial risks, such as sovereign and transfer risks, political risks, such as the possibility of social unrest or the infringement of property rights, and the most significant - macroeconomic risks, such as inflation risk, currency risk, and the risk of a sudden change in the level of taxation, can all be considered as the main elements of country risk 46 .  Existing academic research on the impact of numerous factors on the value of firms, especially those from developing countries, falls into two primary categories. The first collection of publications investigates the effects of the fundamental corporate governance standards in businesses across various nations. The level of corporate governance may serve as a form of barometer for the evolution of the financial and economic systems in various nations, which in turn reflects the particular risks of investing in businesses there 46 . Finding out the relevance of the impact of industry and nation affiliation on the value of businesses and their multiples is another area of  research . S. Heston and K. Ruvenhost conclude that the country affiliation of an organization, as compared to the industry, plays a significant explanatory role in the value of shares after studying nearly a thousand companies from twelve different European countries and incorporating these risks into their model. The authors conclude that diversity through investments in businesses from other industries operating in the same market is less successful than diversification through the distribution of assets among several nations 47 . The work of  K. Erb and K. Harvey  also supports this theory, which states that a company's worth is mostly determined by the economic status of the nation in which it works rather than the overall state of the industry 48 . K. Harvey draws the conclusion that country risks play a substantial influence, while highlighting their special significance for developing nations using the country credit indices of the International Country Risk Handbook. The author also reaches an intriguing conclusion regarding the growing significance of the industry risk factor for businesses operating in developed capital markets, which underlines the importance of paying close attention to and taking into account the country risks of developing nations that are still in the process of transition 48 . The idea of a large impact of corporate governance, including the element of having foreign representation on the board of directors, is validated for the South Korean market in following work by B. Black and J. Hasun 49 .  According to their research, B.  Fox  and  M .  Heller  have found that the corporate governance of Russian enterprises has a considerable impact on the size of their multiples. The authors include the risks of bankruptcy, dilution of equity and profits per share (EPS dilution), non-compliance with information confidentiality, and others as criteria for evaluating the competence of corporate governance 50 .   The research done by K. Erb and K. Harvey using a sample of 117 firms worldwide is regarded as the baseline study for evaluating the influence of nation risks on company multiples. Based on the study's findings, the authors, who looked at the impact of various economic, political, and financial country risks using the example of reverse multiples E/P, BV/P, and Div/P, conclude that the country credit rating has the biggest impact on how much one country's value varies from another 48 . According to the findings of the aforementioned empirical  research , it is crucial to include the nation risk factor when determining the value of businesses, especially those from emerging capital markets. The appropriate balance of risk and return is one of the fundamental concepts of investing. Investors who want to make up for the higher risks brought on by certain characteristics of developing markets might ask for a higher rate of return on their investments. As a result, the values of multiples generated from data from peer businesses in mature capital markets are underestimated when adjusting comparative multiples in the valuation of companies from developing markets to account for these needs 48 . The most common argument appraisers use for altering multiples is market differences, particularly the fact that doing business in an emerging market often differs substantially from doing business in a mature market. There are many techniques for multiple adjusting for changes in market risks, including 5 : relative ratios between multiples;  regression analysis ; government bond rates. Government bond rates.  The approach is based on the premise that the difference in yields to maturity of bonds denominated in the same currency and with the same maturity can serve as an indicator for investors' assessment of the difference in macroeconomic risks between the two  countries. Macroeconomic risks, in turn, can lead to different valuations of two companies with similar economic parameters from a developed and an emerging market 5 .  It seems reasonable that all other things being equal, investors would prefer a company developing under conditions of lower macroeconomic risk, one whose growth is associated with less uncertainty and more stable economic conditions. As a result, investors will value companies from countries with lower macroeconomic risks at higher prices than their emerging market counterparts. Another assumption underlying this approach is that there is a positive relationship between the yield to maturity of a country's government bonds and the probability of default.  According to Ivashkovskaya and Kuznetsov t his strategy can only be used if comparable bonds have equivalent maturities (measured in days). If this is not the case, the yield to maturity must be adjusted such that currency and maturity are equal . Authors  use regression to adjust maturities 4 .  This approach has to take into account the theory of the time dependence of interest rates, which is expressed in a direct relationship between the maturity of the bond and the corresponding spot rates. This dependency is manifested in the positive slope of the yield curve, which accompanies an increase in the annual spot rate.   To derive the equation of the annual ratio between a bond's yield to maturity and its maturity,  the authors  use the method of regression analysis 4 .  The currency in which the bonds are denominated should also be taken into consideration when evaluating the findings (whether or not the adjustment also involves currency risk).   The yield-to-maturity ratio calculated for each year for both developed and emerging market bonds is the correction coefficient that allows country risk to be taken into account 4 . In order to correct the multiple of the developed markets in the future, taking into account the country risks of the emerging markets, it is sufficient to multiply this multiple by the coefficient determined above. This approach has the benefit of being reasonably simple to use and being adaptable to any multiple, regardless of whether it is based on assets or earnings level. The fundamental flaw is that it entirely disregards other unique risks faced by businesses operating in emerging economies. L. Pereiro offers a correction by increasing the median (average) value of the multiple from the reference market by the relative market coefficient of the two nations in his practical book on developing markets. The algorithm utilized in this instance to determine the adjustment  includes   calculation of a  ratio between the median multiple values of the developed and developing market enterprises 51 . A. Damodaran contend s  that data acquired from samples restricted to certain industries yield the most accurate results 22 . This approach is marginally superior to the previous one, since the average multiple for the entire nation takes other risks into account in addition to the standard national risk. Moreover, it may be used with any variety of multiples. The nation where the valuation topic is located must have a securities market, equivalent accounting standards, and a sufficient number of firms to be included for the multiple to be regarded as representative in order for it to be relevant 5 . Regression analysis corrections are based on the notion that a multiple's value is dependent on both the fundamental variable on which it is based and the dummy variable that indicates the nation/market in which commerce is performed. The requirements for using this approach are the same as those for the relative ratio of multiples 5 .  I t may be used by including variables (real or dummy) that correspond to other risk factors like commercial activity, size, growth, etc. This method assumes the presence of fundamental variables that determine the value of certain types of multiples. These variables reflect the risks, rewards and growth potential of companies whose multiples are used to estimate value. The company parameters that potentially affect the value of the multiples are input data for the regression analysis. Of particular interest is the presence of an additional dummy variable when creating the regression model .  If statistically significant, this variable contains a premium that reflects a specific country risk 5 . Error calculation The correctness of valuation multiples should be estimated for a number of reasons, including the following 53 : Value multiples are only projections. They are based on faulty statistics and measurements. The level of uncertainty in the final value estimations may be gauged by analyzing the potential inaccuracies and variability in the multiples. This may influence how accurate the appraisal may be. Many market variables, such as industry standards, growth rates, return on equity, etc., affect valuation multiples. Slight adjustments to these underlying variables can result in very different multiples. It is more helpful to present a range of potential values rather than a single point estimate when multiples' sensitivity to important inputs is estimated. Value multiples are frequently obtained from comparables or peers. Yet, no two businesses can be directly compared. Accurate valuation has an influence on financial and commercial choices. A big potential inaccuracy in a valuation might result in unwise choices. Whether a valuation is accurate enough for the intended usage, it may be determined by estimating accuracy. If not, more research or assessments may be required. By estimating the correctness of multiples, one may show that the valuation was carried out honestly and that important risks and restrictions have been taken into account. As a result, there may be increased trust in analyses, judgments, and choices based on values. In conclusion, assessing precision and accuracy at each stage of the valuation process results in assessments that are more trustworthy and credible overall and enable better business decisions. An essential step in this approach is estimating the valuation multiple error estimates. Efficiency in this context should be interpreted as the least divergence of the multiples of analog ue s acquired values from the provided (actual) values of the multiples of the assessed businesses from developing capital markets after all adjustments. The estimation errors that were acquired after applying each multiple's selection criteria  and adjustments  in full are then calculated. Book value is an accounting measure of a company's assets minus liabilities. It does not necessarily reflect the current market value of assets. The book value can be understated or overstated relative to market value, making P/BV inaccurate. Mean errors for all countries, criteria and multiples show the underestimation of valuation. This may be due to the adjustment effect. Research limitations. The results of an analysis based on only  3  countr ies  may not always be universal due to country specifics. Nevertheless, the main object of research is the  EM . The analysis analyzes a limited number of multiples  and  selection criteria, which may limit investors in finding the most accurate company valuations. However, this study has looked at the most common criteria   and multiples that will suit most investors. Only one combination of selection criteria is used in the analysis, perhaps when considering other combinations, more accurate cases may be found. Nevertheless, the combination of all criteria is most often encountered in practice and in the literature.   The accuracy of multiples-criteria combinations were not examined. A specific company is not compared by different multipliers with a fixed set of peer companies, since different criteria for selecting peer companies are used, which directly affect the choice of peers. CONCLUSION This research is focused on discovering the most accurate multiples  and  selection criteria .    Main results: The most accurate multiples are those based on revenue  (EV/S and P/S) . The least accurate multiple is P/B . The most precise criterion for multiples is  the combined criteria . Hypotheses status: The best selection criteria will be the combination of all criteria  –   approved ; The most accurate multiples will be those based on sales  –  approved ; EV based multiples are more accurate than Price based multiples   –   not  approved . The first hypothesis was confirmed for 2 out of 3 countries, the first hypothesis – for all countries, the third hypothesis – for 1 country. The least accurate multiple for all countries is P/B. No universally the least accurate selection criteria was found. As a result of this research,  the most accurate selection criteria and multiples were explored  which could be used by investors  in order to make accurate evaluations and investment decisions  regarding  EM  companies. This will enable them to make profitable investment decisions.  This research could encourage scholars and practitioners to dive deeper into the topic and analyze the accuracy of different  multiples, selection criteria using  different markets in different time periods.  Moreover, the most accurate combinations of criteria and multiples could be further explored.  This could lead to more reliable universal  results  being discovered. Цели:
1. Оценка влияния различных факторов на финансовые показатели компаний.
2. Анализ сравнительных характеристик компаний разных стран.

Задачи:
1. Выполнить двухкритериевый анализ варьанс (ANOVA) с использованием программного обеспечения JASP для оценки влияния факторов на финансовые показатели компаний.
2. Провести тест на равенство дисперсий (Levene's test) для проверки гипотезы о равенстве дисперсий.
3. Разработать и применять пользовательские контрасты для определения значимых различий между группами компаний.
4. Провести многокритериальный анализ (Multiple comparison) для определения значимых различий между группами компаний по нескольким критериям одновременно.
5. Создать диаграмму распределения (Q-Q plot) для оценки соответствия нормальному распределению данных.

Результаты:
Для бразильских компаний проведены все вышеупомянутые исследования, включая ANOVA, Levene's test, пользовательские контрасты и многокритериальный анализ. Были выявлены значимые различия между группами компаний по некоторым критериям, такие как EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales, P/BV, P/E и P/S.

Для индийских компаний также проведены все вышеупомянутые исследования. Были выявлены значимые различия между группами компаний по некоторым критериям, таким как EV/EBITDA, EV/Sales, P/BV, P/E и P/S. Однако, в отличие от бразильских компаний, для индийских компаний были обнаружены более значительные различия между группами компаний по некоторым критериям.

В целом, результаты показывают, что различные факторы оказывают влияние на финансовые показатели компаний, и что сравнение характеристик компаний разных стран может привести к интересным выводам. Однако, дальнейшее исследование потребует большей глубины и детализации анализа.
